item.title: 理解Burrows-Wheeler Transform的关键性质
item.description: 理解BWT变换过程中，最后一列第i个出现的字符x，与第一列中第i个出现的x是同一个
item.dc:creator: Peter
item.content:encoded:
最近在看bowtie的相关文章和论文，其中提到了FM-index和LF-mapping. 大部分文章和视频只提到了一个BWT的一个关键性质，并没有讲这个属性是怎么来的？或许是太简单了，大家都没有提，但是对于新手来讲，没人讲解的话理解起来就容易摸不到头绪。

在Burrows-Wheeler Matrices中，最后一列第i个出现的字符x，与第一列中第i个出现的x是同一个

怎么理解呢？

从abba$aa，可以看出有4个a。

因为BWT变换是轮转来的，我们把上图的最后一列移位到最开始。

移位之后就变成了这个样子。

会发现变成了Figure 1中的4个a顺序，为什么顺序是一模一样的？那是因为BWT的变换是一个按照字典排序的过程，Figure 2的4组是已经排序好的，在第一列加上相同的a，并不影响排序，也就是排序的结果是一模一样的。

item.title: July Pledge
item.description: a formal promise, or something that is given as a sign that you will keep a promise
item.dc:creator: Peter
item.content:encoded:
Word Type Meaning
ergonomics noun the scientific study of people and their working conditions, especially done in order to improve effectiveness
hot button noun a subject that is important to people and about which they have strong opinions
veteran noun a person who has had a lot of experience of a particular activity
uncover verb to discover something secret or hidden or remove something covering something else
substitute verb to use something or someone instead of another thing or person
reciprocity noun behaviour in which two people or groups of people give each other help and advantages
expedite verb to make something happen more quickly
integrity noun the quality of being honest and having strong moral principles
litigation noun the process of taking a case to a court of law so that a judgment can be made
vigilant adjective being very careful to notice things, esp. signs of danger
ransom noun a large amount of money that is demanded in exchange for someone who has been taken prisoner, or sometimes for an animal
disguise verb to give a new appearance to a person or thing, especially in order to hide its true form
scam noun an illegal plan for making money, especially one that involves tricking people
pledge noun a formal promise, or something that is given as a sign that you will keep a promise

item.title: July Compliance
item.description: the act of obeying an order, rule, or request
item.dc:creator: Peter
item.content:encoded:
Word Type Meaning
incorporate verb to include something as part of something larger
obligation noun a legal or moral duty to do something
attribute noun a quality or characteristic that someone or something has
attribute verb to say or think that something is the result of a particular thing
attribution noun the act of saying what the origin or cause of something is
infringe verb to break a rule, law, etc.
infringement noun an action that breaks a rule, law, etc.
retention noun the continued use or possession of something or someone
retention noun the ability of a company to keep its employees and stop them from going to work somewhere else
retention noun the ability of a company to keep its customers, rather than losing them to competitors
compliance noun the act of obeying an order, rule, or request

item.title: July Proponent
item.description: a person who speaks publicly in support of a particular idea or plan of action
item.dc:creator: Peter
item.content:encoded:
Word Type Meaning
sanitary adjective relating to being clean and healthy, esp. to the protection of health by the removal of dirt and waste, including human waste
barge verb to hurry somewhere or through a place in a rude and forceful way
barge noun a long boat with a flat bottom, used for carrying heavy objects on rivers or canals
aerobics noun energetic physical exercises that make the heart, lungs, and muscles stronger and increase the amount of oxygen in the blood
lateral adjective relating to the sides of an object or plant or to sideways movement
jumble noun an untidy and confused mixture of things, feelings, or ideas
spoil verb to treat someone very well, esp. by being too generous
spoil verb to destroy or damage something, or to become destroyed or damaged
uneasy adjective uncomfortable or anxious
sly adjective seeming to know secrets
sly adjective deceiving people in a clever way in order to get what you want
grin noun a wide smile
deduction noun an amount or part taken away from a total, esp. an expense that you do not have to pay taxes on, or the process of taking away an amount or part
deduction noun the process of learning something by considering a general set of facts and thinking about how something specific relates to them
dividend noun (a part of) the profit of a company that is paid to the people who own shares in it
cosmic adjective relating to the universe and the natural processes that happen in it
extinguisher noun a fire extinguisher
boast verb to speak too proudly or happily about what you have done or what you own
aesthetic adjective relating to the enjoyment or study of beauty
plagiarism noun the process or practice of using another person's ideas or work and pretending that it is your own
gallop verb to move or act quickly
veto noun an official power or right to refuse to accept or allow something
stitch noun a length of special thread used to join the edges of a deep cut in the flesh
statute noun a law that has been formally approved and written down
statue noun an object made from a hard material, especially stone or metal, to look like a person or animal
quench verb to satisfy a need or wish
quench verb to use water to put out a fire
quench verb to drink liquid so that you stop being thirsty
plateau noun a period during which there are no large changes
plateau noun a large flat area of land that is high above sea level
fanatic noun a person who is extremely interested in something, to a degree that some people find unreasonable
humid adjective (of air and weather conditions) containing extremely small drops of water in the air
correspondence noun letters, especially official or business letters
tribute noun something that you say, write, or give that shows your respect and admiration for someone, especially on a formal occasion
stake noun the amount of money that you risk on the result of something such as a game or competition
stake noun a share or a financial involvement in something such as a business
goggle verb to look with the eyes wide open because you are surprised
calibre noun the quality of someone or something, especially someone's ability
tender adjective gentle, loving, or kind
tender noun a written or formal offer to supply goods or do a job for an agreed price
tender verb If you tender for a job, you make a formal offer to do it for a stated price
tender adjective (of meat or vegetables) easy to cut or chew (= crush with the teeth)
tender adjective (of part of the body) painful, sore, or uncomfortable when touched
denote verb to represent something
chink noun a narrow crack or opening
chink verb to make a light ringing sound
stint verb to provide, take, or use only a small amount of something
stint noun a fixed or limited period of time spent doing a particular job or activity
fabricate verb to invent or produce something false in order to deceive someone
intermediate adjective being between two other related things, levels, or points
departmental adjective relating to a department
reclamation noun the treatment of waste materials to get useful materials from them
reclamation noun the attempt to make land suitable for building or farming
burgeon verb to develop or grow quickly
freight noun goods, but not passengers, that are carried from one place to another, by ship, aircraft, train, or truck, or the system of transporting these goods
literally adverb used to emphasize what you are saying
insipid adjective not having a strong taste or character, or having no interest or energy
fibre noun threads when they are in a mass that can be used for making products such as cloth and rope
coast noun the land next to or close to the sea
coarse adjective rough and not smooth or soft, or not in very small pieces
trinket noun a small decorative object, or a piece of jewellery that is cheap or of low quality
coffer noun a large, strong box in which money or valuable objects are kept
moderation noun the quality of doing something within reasonable limits
nominal adjective A nominal amount of money is very small compared to an expected price or value
nominal adjective in name or thought but not in fact or not as things really are
miserable adjective used to emphasize the low quality of value of something
opponent noun a person who disagrees with something and speaks against it or tries to change it
proponent noun a person who speaks publicly in support of a particular idea or plan of action

item.title: June Coalition
item.description: The word coalition connotes a coming together to achieve a goal
item.dc:creator: Peter
item.content:encoded:
Word Type Meaning
ration noun a limited amount of something that one person is allowed to have, especially when there is not much of it available
obstruct verb to block a road, passage, entrance, etc. so that nothing can go along it, or to prevent something from happening correctly by putting difficulties in its way
obscure adjective not known to many people
kneel verb to go down into, or stay in, a position where one or both knees are on the ground
contempt noun a strong feeling of disliking and having no respect for someone or something
subsidiary noun a company that is owned by a larger company
subsidiary adjective used to refer to something less important than something else with which it is connected
presumably adverb used to say what you think is the likely situation
disposal noun the act of getting rid of something, especially by throwing it away
dish noun a container, flatter than a bowl and sometimes with a lid, from which food can be served or which can be used for cooking
dash noun the symbol – used to separate parts of a sentence
dash verb to go somewhere quickly
gross adjective extremely unpleasant
gross adjective (especially in law) unacceptable because clearly wrong
gross adjective extremely fat or large and ugly
gross adjective, adverb (in) total
negligence noun the fact of not giving enough care or attention to someone or something
intestine noun (either of the two parts of) a long tube through which food travels from the stomach and out of the body while it is being digested
consignment noun an amount of goods that is sent somewhere
transcript noun an exact written copy of something
silt noun sand or soil that is carried along by flowing water and then dropped, especially at a bend in a river or at a river's opening
adapt verb to change your ideas or behaviour to make them suitable for a new situation
premises noun the land and buildings owned by someone, especially by a company or organization
premise noun an idea or theory on which a statement or action is based
abode noun the place where someone lives
muddle noun an untidy or confused state
lavatory noun a toilet
discrete adjective clearly separate or different in shape or form
discretion noun the ability to behave without causing embarrassment or attracting too much attention, especially by keeping information secret
discretion noun choice, or the right to make a choice, based on judgment
counsellor noun a lawyer
counsellor noun a person who has been trained to do the job of listening to people who have problems or who want to achieve something, and giving them advice
psychiatric adjective of or relating to the study of mental illness
withstand verb to be strong enough, or not be changed by something, or to oppose a person or thing successfully
exotic adjective unusual and exciting because of coming (or seeming to come) from far away, especially a tropical country
stuffy adjective old-fashioned, formal, and boring
stuffy adjective A stuffy room or building is unpleasant because it has no fresh air
entitle verb to give a title to a book, film, etc.
entitle verb to give someone the right to do or have something
inquest noun an official examination of facts in an attempt to discover the cause of something, esp. of a sudden or violent death
exalt verb to praise someone a lot, or to raise someone to a higher rank or more powerful position
oath noun a promise, especially that you will tell the truth in a law court
unbecoming adjective not suitable or acceptable
renounce verb to say formally or publicly that you no longer own, support, believe in, or have a connection with something
ethics noun the study of what is morally right and wrong, or a set of beliefs about what is morally right and wrong
footage noun film or videotape that shows a single event or place
scour verb to search a place or thing very carefully in order to try to find something
coalition noun the joining together of different political parties or groups for a particular purpose, usually for a limited time, or a government that is formed in this way
coalition noun a group of people or organizations working together to achieve something

item.title: Jun Dynamic
item.description: Economic dynamics is the study of an economy in which rates of output are changing
item.dc:creator: Peter
item.content:encoded:
Word Type Meaning
asylum noun protection or safety, especially that given by a government to people who have been forced to leave their own countries for their safety or because of war
inquiry noun An inquiry is also an official attempt to discover the facts about something.
scrutiny noun the careful and detailed examination of something in order to get information about it
signature adjective used to describe the product or service that a company or person is known for
sentiment noun a thought, opinion, or idea based on a feeling about a situation, or a way of thinking about something
clash verb to fight or argue
clash verb If two opinions, statements, or qualities clash, they are very different from each other
intelligence noun secret information about the governments of other countries, especially enemy governments, or a group of people who collect and deal with this information
grave adjective seriously bad
sceptical adjective doubting that something is true or useful
rational adjective based on clear thought and reason
eclipse noun a situation in which something becomes less important
flatter verb to praise someone in order to make them feel attractive or important, sometimes in a way that is not sincere
susceptible adjective easily influenced or harmed by something
deliberately adverb intentionally
sanction verb to formally give permission for something
sanction noun an official order, such as the stopping of trade, that is taken against a country in order to make it obey international law
magnify verb to make something look larger than it is, especially by looking at it through a lens
liable adjective likely to do, happen, or experience something
liable adjective having (legal) responsibility for something or someone
hormone noun any of various chemicals made by living cells that influence the development, growth, sex, etc. of an animal and are carried around the body in the blood
succeed verb to come after another person or thing in time
precede verb to be or go before something or someone in time or space
dynamic noun the forces that control the relationships people or things have with each other and how those relationships can change
dynamic adjective having a lot of ideas and enthusiasm
play noun a piece of writing that is intended to be acted in a theatre or on radio or television
play verb to perform an entertainment or a particular character in a play, film, etc.
commission noun a group of people who have been formally chosen to discover information about a problem or examine the reasons why the problem exists
commission verb to give someone the official authority to be an officer in the armed forces
commission verb to formally choose someone to do a special piece of work, or to formally ask for a special piece of work from someone
hay noun grass that is cut and dried and used as animal food
dwarf noun a person who is much smaller than the usual size
dwarf noun in stories for children, a creature like a little man with magical powers
stall noun a small closed area within a farm building in which there is space for one animal to be kept
stall noun a large table or a small shop with an open front from which goods are sold in a public place
neat adjective tidy, with everything in its place
tidy adjective having everything ordered and arranged in the right place, or liking to keep things like this
shelter noun (a building designed to give) protection from bad weather, danger, or attack
magnificent adjective very good, beautiful, or deserving to be admired
prosperous adjective successful, usually by earning a lot of money
aggregate noun something formed by adding together several amounts or things
aggravate verb to make a bad situation worse
opulence noun the quality of being expensive and luxurious
fact sheet noun a written document containing information for the public
conception noun an idea of what something or someone is like, or a basic understanding of a situation or a principle
vile adjective unpleasant, immoral, and unacceptable
slander noun a false spoken statement about someone that damages their reputation, or the making of such a statement
sketch noun a simple, quickly-made drawing that does not have many details
slash verb to cut with a sharp blade using a quick, strong movement
wholesome adjective good for you, and likely to improve your life either physically, morally, or emotionally
ruthless adjective not thinking or worrying about any pain caused to others; cruel
thrifty adjective showing a careful use of money, especially by avoiding waste
brazen adjective obvious, without any attempt to be hidden
demography noun the study of changes in the number of births, marriages, deaths, etc. in a particular area during a period of time
dramatic adjective very sudden or noticeable, or full of action and excitement
gorgeous adjective very beautiful or pleasant
gorge noun a deep, narrow valley with steep sides, usually formed by a river or stream cutting through hard rock
sensational adjective Sensational news reports and articles are intended to be shocking and exciting rather than serious
sensational adjective very good, exciting, or unusual
sensation noun something very exciting or interesting, or something that causes great excitement or interest
urgent adjective very important and needing attention immediately
urgency noun the quality of being very important and needing attention immediately
hinge noun a piece of metal that fastens the edge of a door, window, lid, etc. to something else and allows it to open or close
advocacy noun public support for an idea, plan, or way of doing something
craft noun skill and experience, especially in relation to making objects; a job or activity that needs skill and experience, or something produced using skill and experience
affliction noun something that makes you suffer
aspiration noun something that you hope to achieve
rapport noun a good understanding of someone and an ability to communicate well with them
consolidate verb to bring together or unite things that were separate
attainable adjective possible to achieve
trendy adjective modern and influenced by the most recent fashions or ideas
reiterate verb to say something again, once or several times
retreat verb If a price retreats, it goes down after it has gone up
retreat verb to decide not to do something, or to stop believing something, because it causes too many problems
retreat verb to go to a quiet safe place in order to avoid a difficult situation

item.title: June Dedicated
item.description: Devoted to a cause, ideal, or purpose
item.dc:creator: Peter
item.content:encoded:
Word Type Meaning
infant noun a baby or a very young child
indigenous adjective existing naturally or having always lived in a place; native
logistics noun the careful organization of a complicated activity so that it happens in a successful and effective way
account for sth to form part of a total
mandate noun authority to act in a particular way given to a government or a person, esp. as a result of a vote or ruling
accountable adjective Someone who is accountable is completely responsible for what they do and must be able to give a satisfactory reason for it
practical adjective able to provide effective solutions to problems
practical adjective suitable for the situation in which something is used
practical adjective relating to experience, real situations, or actions rather than ideas or imagination
barely adverb by the smallest amount
acclaim noun public approval and praise
in jeopardy noun in danger of being damaged or destroyed
alleged adjective said or thought by some people to be the stated bad or illegal thing, although you have no proof
breach noun an act of breaking a law, promise, agreement, or relationship
liable adjective very likely to do something
liable adjective having (legal) responsibility for something or someone
delicate adjective needing careful treatment, especially because easily damaged
dedicated adjective designed to be used for one particular purpose
dedicated adjective believing that something is very important and giving a lot of time and energy to it

item.title: Jun Conviction
item.description: A conviction is something certain: a judgment of guilty in court and a strong belief are both convictions.
item.dc:creator: Peter
item.content:encoded:
Word Type Meaning
opulent adjective expensive and luxurious
incongruous adjective unusual or different from what is around or from what is generally happening
heir noun a person who will legally receive money, property, or a title from another person, especially an older member of the same family, when that other person dies
apportion verb to give or share something among several people or things
slumber noun sleep
meagre adjective very small in amount or number or not enough in amount or number
perpetuate verb to cause something to continue
slacken verb to (cause to) become loose
teeming adjective filled with the activity of many people or things
teeming adjective If a place is teeming, it is full of people
the brunt of sth noun the main force of something unpleasant
provoke verb to cause a reaction, especially a negative one
provoke verb to make or try to make a person or an animal angry
unobtrusive adjective not noticeable; seeming to fit in well with the things around
stove noun a piece of equipment that burns fuel or uses electricity in order to heat a place
resent verb to dislike or be angry at something or someone because you have been hurt or not treated fairly
conviction noun a strong opinion or belief
conviction noun the fact of officially being found to be guilty of a particular crime, or the act of officially finding someone guilty
